summary: American author best known for the ambiguous short story 'The Lady, or the Tiger?' Its sequel is 'The Discourager of Hesitancy,' in which the title object is a sword.

works.name: "The Lady, or the Tiger?"
works.description: A famous short story with an ambiguous ending. It concerns a punishment given to a peasant for being the lover of a princess: he must choose between two doors, behind one of which is a lady (whom he would marry) and behind the other a tiger. The story never reveals which door the peasant opens. Its sequel, 'The Discourager of Hesitancy,' features the title object, which is a sword (or scimitar).

clues.clue: Short story without a clear ending about a peasant punished for being the princess's lover
examples: this short story without a clear ending. It discusses a punishment given to a peasant for being the lover of a princess

clues.clue: Sequel 'The Discourager of Hesitancy' — the title object is a sword
examples: A sequel to this story is entitled 'The Discourager of Hesitancy'... The title 'Discourager of Hesitancy' is this kind of weapon

comprehensive_summary: Frank R. Stockton (Frank Richard Stockton) was an American author whose clue presence is almost entirely limited to a single short story, 'The Lady, or the Tiger?' This famous story presents an unresolved dilemma: a peasant who has been the lover of a princess must choose between two doors — behind one is a lady he would marry, and behind the other is a tiger that would kill him. The story famously never reveals which door is opened. Its sequel, 'The Discourager of Hesitancy,' features the title object, which is identified as a sword or scimitar.

cards.front: Short Story: a peasant punished for being the princess's lover must choose between two doors — one hiding a lady, the other a tiger — and the ending is never resolved
cards.back: "The Lady, or the Tiger?" (Frank R. Stockton)

cards.front: Short Story: the sequel to 'The Lady, or the Tiger?' is titled for this object, which is a sword
